figures.caption: Figure 2: Comparison of Kalman filtering in the original space and in an RKHS. Through kernel embedding µ, the state vector x(t) and its covariance P(t) are lifted to the RKHS mean mt and covariance operator St, with •− and •+ denoting the prior and posterior. We utilize linear matrices F and H (instead of nonlinear transitions ptr and pob) to provide better intuition.
Figure 3: Schematic of the ELTO-AKF approach with dynamic parameter adaptation.
Table 1: Comparison of sequential state estimation methods.

Table 2: Dynamic state estimation performance (MSE (×10−2)) on piecewise non-stationary LiDAR trajectories. We compare the baseline ELTO-KF with our ELTO-AKF under two settings: full optimization and pure adaptation. In the former setting, the initial noise covariance matrices are tuned (using CMA-ES) with α = β = 1. In the latter, they are tuned purely through dynamic parameter adaptation (α = β = 0.9), without using CMA-ES.

Table 6: Comparison of denoising methods based on the ELTO-KF and our proposed ELTO-AKF with varying memory factors. Here, the correct viscosity is 0.01 π . Note that the identified PDE is incorrect when no denoising is applied; therefore, its evaluation metrics are unavailable (N/A).

Figure 4: The state-variable data are depicted under three conditions: the clean data, the noisy (ϵ = 50) observed data, and the denoised data produced by the ELTO-AKF, which shows the smallest MSE to the clean data.
Figure 5: Recovered Lotka–Volterra models from the lynx–hare data. The trajectory-fit solution and the smoothed observations closely match the simulated solution from the reference coefficients.
Table 10: MSE under varying adaptation rates (α = β). Results are grouped by matched (MSE ×10−2) and mismatched (MSE ×10−1) noise conditions.
